nativeRange.habitat: Sandy areas of shallow lagoons and protected coastal bays, sharing burrows with alpheid pistol shrimps

decorPreferences: fine to medium sand bed for burrowing
loose rubble and live rock to form burrow structure

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty marine foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, chopped table shrimp, and frozen preparations for carnivores. Best offered small meals once or twice daily; target-feeding near the burrow helps shy individuals get their share.

avoidWith: other shrimpgobies competing for the same burrow
conspecifics outside of a mated pair

summary: The Yellow Watchman Goby is a hardy, peaceful saltwater fish native to the Western Pacific, well known for forming a mutualistic partnership with pistol shrimps. It is widely regarded as one of the most beginner-friendly marine gobies available.
careNotes: The Yellow Watchman Goby (Cryptocentrus cinctus) is commonly considered one of the most beginner-friendly saltwater fish: generally hardy, peaceful, and reef-safe with a bright yellow body and an engaging personality. It spends most of its time perched at or just inside a burrow it shares with an alpheid pistol shrimp — most often Alpheus bellulus, the Tiger Pistol Shrimp, though other Alpheus species pair readily. The shrimp digs and maintains the burrow while the goby, with its superior vision, keeps watch and alerts the nearly-blind shrimp to approaching threats via tail-flicks; both animals retreat into the burrow when alarmed. Pairing the goby with a compatible pistol shrimp is strongly recommended and tends to produce one of the most engaging symbiotic displays available in the marine hobby. Provide a fine-to-medium sand bed at least 5 cm deep along with loose rubble or rockwork the pair can excavate beneath. A tight-fitting lid is important — the Yellow Watchman Goby has been known to jump, especially when startled or when competing with conspecifics. It is generally tolerant of other peaceful tankmates, but tends to be territorial toward other shrimpgobies and its own kind unless introduced as an established mated pair. Captive-bred individuals are increasingly available and tend to acclimate more readily than wild-caught specimens.
breedingNotes: The Yellow Watchman Goby has been bred in captivity, though commercial captive-bred availability is still limited. It forms mated pairs that share a burrow and has been reported to spawn on the substrate, with the male guarding the eggs.

fish.breedingNotes: Mated pairs share a burrow and have been reported to spawn on the substrate, with the male providing egg care. Captive breeding has been achieved but remains uncommon at commercial scale.
